title: 1553rd meeting (9-11 March 2026) (DH) - H46-10 Sejdić and Finci group v. Bosnia and Herzegovina (Application No. 27996/06)
text: 5 CM/Notes/1553/H46-10 MINISTERS’ DEPUTIES Notes on the Agenda CM/Notes/1553/H46-10 11 March 2026 1553rd meeting, 9-11 March 2026 (DH) Human rights H46-10 Sejdić and Finci group v. Bosnia and Herzegovina (Application No. 27996/06) Supervision of the execution of the European Court’s judgments Reference documents DH-DD(2026)214, CM/Del/Dec(2025)1545/H46-9 Application Case Judgment of Final on Indicator for the classification 27996/06 SEJDIĆ AND FINCI 22/12/2009 Grand Chamber Complex problem 3681/06 ZORNIĆ 15/07/2014 15/12/2014 56666/12 ŠLAKU 26/05/2016 26/05/2016 41939/07 PILAV 09/06/2016 09/09/2016 Case description These cases concern discrimination against the applicants on account of their ineligibility to stand for election to the Presidency of Bosnia and Herzegovina due to their lack of affiliation with a constituent people (i.e. Bosniaks, Croats or Serbs) or due to their failure to meet a combination of the requirements of ethnic origin and place of residence (violations of Article 1 of Protocol No. 12). In accordance with the Constitution of Bosnia and Herzegovina, only persons declaring affiliation with a “constituent people” are entitled to stand for election to the Presidency, which consists of three members: one Bosniak and one Croat, each directly elected from the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina, and one Serb directly elected from the Republika Srpska. The applicants in these cases were ineligible to stand for election to the Presidency of Bosnia and Herzegovina because, in the case of Sejdić and Finci, they are of Roma The term “Roma and Travellers” is used at the Council of Europe to encompass the wide diversity of the groups covered by the work of the Council of Europe in this field: on the one hand a) Roma, Sinti/Manush, Calé, Kaale, Romanichals, Boyash/Rudari; b) Balkan Egyptians (Egyptians and Ashkali); c) Eastern groups (Dom, Lom and Abdal); and, on the other hand, groups such as Travellers, Yenish, and the populations designated under the administrative term “Gens du voyage”, as well as persons who identify themselves as Gypsies. The present is an explanatory footnote, not a definition of Roma and/or Travellers. and Jewish origin, in Šlaku and Zornić, of Albanian and undeclared origin respectively, and in Pilav, because the applicant is a Bosniak living in the Republika Srpska. At its 1501st DH meeting (June 2024), the Committee of Ministers decided to close the examination of individual measures in the case of Pudarić, given that the applicant had passed away. The case concerned discrimination of a Serb living in the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina. The cases (apart from Pilav) also concern violations of the right to free elections and discrimination against the applicants who were ineligible to stand for election to the House of Peoples of Bosnia and Herzegovina due to their lack of affiliation with a constituent people (a violation of Article 14 taken in conjunction with Article 3 of Protocol No. 1). In Zornić and Šlaku, the Court under Article 46 of the Convention, noted that “[i]n Sejdić and Finci the Court observed that when the impugned constitutional provisions were put in place a very fragile ceasefire was in effect on the ground and that the provisions were designed to end a brutal conflict marked by genocide and “ethnic cleansing”. The nature of the conflict was such that the approval of the “constituent peoples” was necessary to ensure peace. However, now, more than eighteen years after the end of the tragic conflict, there could no longer be any reason for the maintenance of the contested constitutional provisions. The Court expects that democratic arrangements will be made without further delay. In view of the need to ensure effective political democracy, the Court considers that the time has come for a political system which will provide every citizen of Bosnia and Herzegovina with the right to stand for elections to the Presidency and the House of Peoples of Bosnia and Herzegovina without discrimination based on ethnic affiliation and without granting special rights for constituent people to the exclusion of minorities or citizens of Bosnia and Herzegovina.” Status of execution 1) Necessity to amend the Constitution and the electoral legislation To fully enforce the judgments of the European Court, it is necessary to amend the Constitution of Bosnia and Herzegovina as well as the electoral legislation. However, for these amendments to be adopted, the country’s political leaders need to reach an agreement on their scope and content. 2) The Committee of Ministers’ examination of this group of cases The Committee has been following this group very closely since the European Court delivered the leading Grand Chamber judgment in 2009. Notwithstanding the Committee’s interventions and the extensive support offered to the authorities by both the Council of Europe and the European Union, four general elections have been held under discriminatory rules, in 2010, 2014, 2018 and 2022. The Committee has so far used a wide range of tools with a view to addressing the situation. This has included the adoption of five interim resolutions (in 2011, 2012, 2013, 2021 and 2023); the issuing of a declaration by the Chair; See document CM(2010)59-rev Declaration on Bosnia and Herzegovina, by Micheline Calmy-Rey, outgoing Chair of the Committee of Ministers, and Antonio Miloshoski, incoming Chair of the Committee of Ministers (120th Session of the Committee of Ministers, 11 May 2010). the examination of these judgments at each of the Committee’s Human Rights meetings; See Interim Resolution CM/ResDH(2012)233, adopted at the 1157th meeting (December 2012) (DH). sending a letter from the Chair; See decision adopted at the 1172nd meeting (June 2013) (DH). inviting member States, observer States and the European Union to raise the case in their contacts with Bosnia and Herzegovina; See decisions adopted at the 1273rd meeting (December 2016) (DH), 1288th meeting (June 2017) (DH), 1324th meeting (September 2018) (DH), 1428th meeting (March 2022) (DH), and 1521st meeting (March 2025) (DH). and inviting the competent Minister for an exchange of views on the strategy for these cases. See decisions adopted at the 1172nd meeting (June 2013) (DH), 1324th meeting (September 2018) (DH), 1348th meeting (June 2019) (DH), Interim Resolution CM/ResDH(2023)481, adopted at the 1483rd meeting (December 2023) (DH), and decision adopted at the 1501st meeting (June 2024) (DH). Furthermore, other bodies of the Council of Europe have also repeatedly called on the authorities to execute this group of cases without delay, including the Secretary General of the Council of Europe, For example, in 2012 joint statement with the Commissioner for Enlargement and European Neighbourhood Policy Štefan Füle, and in a 2020 letter addressed to the Deputy Chair of the Council of Ministers and Minister for Foreign Affairs of Bosnia and Herzegovina. the Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe, For example, in Resolution 1701 (2010), Resolution 1725 (2010), Resolution 1855 (2012), Recommendation 2025 (2013), Resolution 2201 (2018), Resolution 2574 (2024), and Resolution 2638 (2026). and the Council of Europe Commissioner for Human Rights. For example, in her Opinion published in 2020 focusing on the situation in Bosnia and Herzegovina 25 years following the Dayton Accords, and her 2023 communication to the Committee of Ministers (DH-DD(2023)552). 3) The Committee’s last examination of this group In its decision adopted at the 1545th meeting (December 2025) (DH), the Committee deeply deplored that despite the repeated assurances by the authorities over the past 16 years (including in the coalition agreement of 29 November 2022 between the leaders of several political parties), the constitutional and legislative amendments required by these judgments had still not been made and time was running out for the authorities to bring the Constitution and electoral legislation of Bosnia and Herzegovina in compliance with the Convention before the forthcoming 2026 elections. It welcomed the consultations that took place in Sarajevo in September 2025 between the Directorate General of Human Rights and the Rule of Law of the Council of Europe and the authorities and political representatives in Bosnia and Herzegovina, as well as the commitment expressed by all parties during these consultations to the full implementation of this group. It noted with interest the adoption of the revised action plan on the execution of this group of cases and the establishment of a Working Group The Working Group consists of 12 members - appointed by the Parliamentary Assembly of Bosnia and Herzegovina, the Council of Ministers, the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina and Republika Srpska – and includes representatives of both the governing coalition and opposition parties, as well as non-party members. tasked with preparing draft amendments to the Constitution of Bosnia and Herzegovina and the Election Law. It also welcomed the Working Group’s first meeting in November 2025, at which it adopted the necessary procedural decisions, as well as its meeting in Strasbourg on 1 December 2025 with Council of Europe experts, and its decision to present and examine concrete proposals at its next meeting in January 2026. The Committee exhorted the authorities to pursue this process without further delay and in good faith, giving priority at this stage to the execution of this group of cases over other ancillary issues. It further urged the authorities to redouble their efforts with a view to presenting to the Parliamentary Assembly of Bosnia and Herzegovina, by early 2026, concrete proposals of the reforms necessary to bring the electoral system into full compliance with the Convention requirements, and invited them to keep it regularly informed, on a monthly basis, of all relevant developments in the electoral reform process. It also affirmed its readiness, in the absence of tangible progress, to consider at the present meeting taking new action aimed at strengthening their political dialogue and co-operation with the authorities of Bosnia and Herzegovina to ensure the effective execution of these judgments. 4) Information provided by the authorities In his communication dated 6 February (DH-DD(2026)214) the Minister of Justice of Bosnia and Herzegovina reported that the Working Group held a meeting on 21 January and that, by that date, three draft proposals for the amendments to the Constitution of Bosnia and Herzegovina – described as being “in line with the positions set out in the judgments of the European Court” – had been received from its members. As one of the proponents was not present at the above meeting, however, the Working Group had not discussed any of the proposals. The next meeting of the Group was scheduled for 18 February, at which the received proposals, as well as any others that might be submitted by the members in the meantime, are to be discussed. 5) Rule 9 communications In her Rule 9.1 communication dated 4 February 2026 (DH-DD(2026)203) the applicant, Ms. Zornić, requested that that the Committee, without delay, take all legal and political measures at its disposal to urge the authorities of Bosnia and Herzegovina to adopt the necessary constitutional and electoral reforms prior to the calling and holding of the 2026 elections, so to enable her, as well as all other citizens in the same or similar position, to stand as candidates for the Presidency of Bosnia and Herzegovina. In their joint Rule 9.2 communication dated 27 January 2026 (DH-DD(2026)198), Human Rights Watch and Minority Rights Group called, inter alia, on the Committee to maintain the pressure on the Council of Ministers of Bosnia and Herzegovina to adopt, in time for the October 2026 elections, the measures necessary to implement the Court’s judgments and to establish a clear timeline for their implementation, outlining specific milestones and firm deadlines for every task force, working group, or other body designated by the government to carry out the required reforms. They noted that the Working Group could be legitimate and effective only if the process ensured the meaningful participation of minorities. They also urged the Committee to consider imposing sanctions on Bosnia and Herzegovina for prolonged failure to abide by its international obligations. 6) Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe In a resolution adopted on 27 January 2026, the Parliamentary Assembly, inter alia, underlined that upon accession Bosnia and Herzegovina has committed to review, with the assistance of the Venice Commission, its electoral legislation in the light of Council of Europe standards, and to revise it where necessary. It recalled the need for constitutional reform in the country to implement the present group of judgments. The Assembly welcomed the creation of a Working Group for the preparation of amendments to the Constitution and the Election Law and urged the political parties to find an agreement on these amendments to bring the constitutional and legislative framework into line with the requirements of the European Convention on Human Rights. Lastly, the Assembly encouraged the authorities to pursue their co-operation with the Council of Europe, in particular the Venice Commission, and make use of its expertise for the electoral and constitutional reform. Resolution 2638 (2026), adopted on 27 January 2026. 7) Proceedings before the European Court of Human Rights On 3 February 2026, the European Court delivered judgments in Begić v. Bosnia and Herzegovina (5067/23) and Begić v. Bosnia and Herzegovina (No. 2) (16613/23), which are not yet final. In these cases the European Court found violations of the general prohibition of discrimination under Article 1 of Protocol No. 12, insofar as the applicant was ineligible to stand for election to the position of Chair/Deputy Chair of the House of Representatives of the Parliamentary Assembly of Bosnia and Herzegovina and to the position of President/Vice-President of the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina respectively, all on account of not declaring affiliation with constitutionally defined “constituent peoples”. Both violations find their origin in the constitutions of Bosnia and Herzegovina and the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina respectively. Analysis of the Secretariat Despite the repeated assurances by the authorities of Bosnia and Herzegovina over the past 16 years (including in the coalition agreement of 29 November 2022 between the leaders of several political parties), the necessary constitutional and legislative amendments have still not been introduced. As the Committee has already underlined, See decisions adopted at the 1172nd meeting (June 2013) (DH), 1324th meeting (September 2018) (DH), 1348th meeting (June 2019) (DH); 1369th meeting (March 2020)(DH); 1383rd meeting (September 2020)(DH); 1398th meeting (March 2021) (DH); 1521st meeting (March 2025), 1545th meeting (December 2025)(DH), as well as Interim Resolutions CM/ResDH(2013)259 and CM/ResDH(2021)427. the retention of the present discriminatory election system constitutes a clear violation of the requirements of the European Convention on Human Rights and a manifest breach of Bosnia and Herzegovina’s unconditional obligation under Article 46 of the Convention to abide by the Court’s judgments, central to its undertakings as a member State of the Council of Europe. Against this background, the Minister of Justice of Bosnia and Herzegovina, who is also the Chair of the Working Group, recently reported that the Group had held a meeting on 21 January, at which it took note of the three received proposals for constitutional reforms, but did not yet discuss them. The next meeting is scheduled for 18 February, at which these and possibly other proposals, are to be discussed. The authorities have so far neither requested expert support from the Council of Europe regarding the mentioned proposals – for example, from the Venice Commission or the Council of Europe experts who participated in the Group’s Strasbourg session in December 2025 – nor provided them to the Committee for possible assessment. Although it is encouraging that the Working Group continues to meet, at the time of the preparation of the present notes no substantive discussions on the actual proposals had yet taken place, while the time available to bring the Constitution and electoral legislation of Bosnia and Herzegovina in compliance with the Convention before the upcoming October 2026 elections is rapidly running out. In this connection, it is recalled that the Committee has exhorted the authorities to pursue the process of the necessary reforms without further delay and in good faith, giving priority at this stage to the execution of this group of cases over other ancillary issues. The authorities were urged to redouble their efforts to present to the Parliamentary Assembly of Bosnia and Herzegovina, by early 2026, concrete proposals regarding the reforms necessary to bring the electoral system into compliance with the Convention requirements. To that end, they were strongly encouraged to take advantage of all expertise within the Council of Europe, including the Venice Commission. The European Court’s judgments of 3 February 2026 in Begić and Begić (No. 2) are not final and therefore have no legal effect at this stage, Under Articles 43 and 44 of the Convention, this Chamber judgment is not final. During the three-month period following its delivery, any party may request that the case be referred to the Grand Chamber of the Court. If such a request is made, a panel of five judges considers whether the case deserves further examination. In that event, the Grand Chamber will hear the case and deliver a final judgment. If the referral request is refused, the Chamber judgment will become final on that day. although, upon becoming final, they could affect the scope of required constitutional reforms. Nevertheless, in view of the long-standing requirements of the present group of cases and the urgent need to resolve them, the authorities would be well advised to continue with their present reflections, while also taking into account the possible implications of the Begić and Begić (No. 2) cases. At its last examination, the Committee decided to consider taking new action at the present meeting in the absence of tangible progress. It is recalled that a summary of the means at the Committee’s disposal in the context of its supervision of the execution of judgments of the Court is contained in document DH-DD(2024)330. Financing assured: YES
case_name: Sejdić and Finci v. Bosnia and Herzegovina [GC]
